articles.title: 5 Notoriously Unreliable Retro Consoles You Should Avoid
articles.author: staff@bgr.com (Aaron Greenbaum)
articles.content:
5 Notoriously Unreliable Retro Consoles You Should Avoid
The landscape of modern games is full of uninspired sequels, season passes, and microtransactions. Is it any wonder why many people love to play retro titles? Well, that and nostalgia. While many retro enthusiasts would rather emulate games, some prefer a more authentic experience with a retro console.
Consoles such as the Nintendo Entertainment System and Sega Genesis defined the 1980s, but time flies so quickly that even the Xbox 360 and PlayStation 3 are now considered retro. Many of these retro consoles were considered cutting-edge back in their day, and some still function in 2026. However, other retro consoles have earned a notorious reputation for rotting away before our eyes. For instance, the original Nintendo Game Boy's screen clarity has degraded considerably because its dot-matrix screen breaks down over time. The rest of your old Game Boy is mostly reliable, but the same cannot be said for other retro gaming platforms, some of which are ticking time bombs unless you know how to repair them or send them to people who do. Heck, some of these consoles were powder kegs back when they were released.
Xbox 360
Many gamers argue that the Xbox 360 was the best console Microsoft ever produced. But early models were plagued by one of the most infamous console issues ever: the Red Ring of Death.
The Red Ring of Death occurred when launch model 360s either froze up or refused to boot, always followed by the front lights flashing red and forming a ring, hence its name. The cause can be traced back to the console's development, which was rushed to get it on store shelves before the PlayStation 3. The connection to the GPU has an inherent flaw: repeated heating and cooling cycles stress the connection until it breaks. Subsequent models fixed the issue, but all release versions are unintentional powder kegs. As a result, you're better off using a late-gen version to play Xbox 360-exclusive titles.
Ironically, early PlayStation 3 models (both the fat and slim) have a similar issue: when you try to turn the console on, the front light may flash yellow and then switch to red. This issue, known as the "Yellow Light of Death," signals a general hardware failure. While not as widespread as the Red Ring of Death, the Yellow Light of Death is spreading among many PS3s as they age. The most likely culprit is the early models' NEC/TOKIN capacitors, which have short lifespans. If true, most PS3s are living on borrowed time, so if you want to watch DVDs on the console, use a later model that relies on multi-layer ceramic capacitors instead.
Xbox
The original Xbox was a flawed but important first step for Microsoft into the video game industry. A complete — but used — OG Xbox can fetch prices as high as $750 in today's market, which is a lot of money to spend on a device that can destroy itself.
The key issue with original Xboxes is the clock capacitor. This component keeps the internal clock running after the console is unplugged. For five hours, anyway. However, most of these capacitors contain a dangerous design flaw: they are prone to leaking acid, which slowly eats away at the motherboard. If you own one of these consoles, it doesn't matter whether it is plugged in or in storage; your Xbox is slowly but surely rotting away as you read this sentence.
If you are adamant about owning an OG Xbox and want to keep it in working condition, you have to open it up and replace the clock capacitor. The words "powerstor aerogel" are stamped on it, so it's easy enough to locate. However, Microsoft stopped using these faulty components with the 1.6 revision — these are the only models that aren't prone to capacitor leakage. If you find an Xbox in a thrift store, you can determine its version by flipping the console over and looking at the stickers on the bottom. If the date next to "MFG. DATE" is after "14/03/2004" and/or the serial numbers are listed as "LNNNNNN 41WFF" or "LNNNNNN 43WFF," the console is safe. If not, either don't buy it or replace the clock capacitor immediately after purchase.
Sega Dreamcast
The Sega Dreamcast was Sega's last hurrah before the company abandoned hardware to focus on software. Many gamers considered the console ahead of its time, but the Dreamcast wasn't a stranger to odd issues, such as a nasty malware infection found on some Dreamcast discs.
You can also brick the console just by using the controllers. The Sega Dreamcast has a unique flaw: plugging in, unplugging, or even just wiggling the controller cable can blow the fuse. If that happens, the Dreamcast will refuse to accept any controller inputs. Thankfully, this error can only occur when the Dreamcast is powered on, but it is still a huge oversight, since you could swap controller ports with every other console at the time. Heck, you can't even damage Psycho Mantis in "Metal Gear Solid" unless you swap controller ports on the PlayStation. Imagine if Sony had ported the game to the Dreamcast, you tried the same tactic, and the console couldn't recognize your controller anymore because you blew a fuse!
Unlike other consoles, the Dreamcast's faulty fuse isn't time-sensitive, so make sure the controller plug remains stable while in use if you play on original hardware. But given how intense some games can get (especially "Jet Set Radio" or "Crazy Taxi"), that's easier said than done. You can replace the controller port fuse with a more stable alternative with some technical know-how, but if you are all thumbs, you might be better off purchasing modern ports of Dreamcast titles.
Atari Jaguar
The Atari Jaguar was touted as the world's first 64-bit console, but it failed due to a litany of problems, including a poor library of games. And if you thought reviews were unkind back in the day, time has been even worse.
Many of the Atari Jaguar's issues can be traced to its cartridge bus. First off, the console doesn't turn on without a game inserted. That is a design flaw in and of itself, and it only gets worse when you realize the console doesn't include any flaps to deter dust buildup. Keeping the cartridge bus clean is a veritable exercise in futility, and once the connector goes, so does the console. And that's just the base model.
To help keep the console alive, Atari released an add-on, the Atari Jaguar CD, that could play CD-based titles. However, this device was poorly designed and prone to malfunctions. Nobody knows for sure why, but the general consensus is that this add-on was shoddily built and couldn't reliably connect to the base Atari Jaguar. In some cases, the Atari Jaguar CD refused to function even when directly hardwired into the base Atari Jaguar. If you are ever in the mood for playing a retro Atari console, you're better off looking for the much rarer Atari Cosmos.
Sega Game Gear
The Nintendo Game Boy dominated the handheld gaming market. Unlike prior handheld platforms, you could swap games, making the Game Boy the first portable cartridge-based device to hit store shelves. Sega tried to follow suit with its own handheld, the Sega Game Gear, but it couldn't compete.
It also didn't help that the Game Gear shipped with faulty components. As with the original Xbox, the main issue lies in the capacitors. The build quality of these components leaves a lot to be desired; combined with the age of Game Gears today, they are bound to fail eventually. Virtually every function of the Game Gear utilizes multiple of these faulty capacitors, and if they don't fail outright, they leak. This means any Game Gears you have in storage are probably being eaten away from the inside out.
The screen is another common problem among Game Gears. This component is more advanced than the Game Boy's because it displays colors and includes a backlight, but the backlight is also a huge power hog. Frequent use can also toast the display driver circuits, rendering the screen unusable. Good luck correctly diagnosing this, though. The aforementioned bad capacitors can also impact screen visibility. Even if your Game Gear works today, the console's overreliance on capacitors makes repairing it an expensive hassle because the different problems often share the same symptoms.
articles.summary:
Collectors and retro gaming enthusiasts should exercise caution when purchasing older hardware, as several iconic consoles from the 1980s to the 2000s suffer from critical, long-term hardware failures.

### Key Points
- Early Xbox 360 models are prone to the "Red Ring of Death" caused by GPU connection failures from thermal stress.
- Original Xbox consoles often suffer from leaking clock capacitors that can permanently corrode the motherboard if not replaced.
- PlayStation 3 units face the "Yellow Light of Death" due to failing NEC/TOKIN capacitors in early hardware revisions.
- Sega Dreamcast consoles feature a fragile controller port fuse that can blow if cables are moved while the system is powered.
- The Sega Game Gear relies on low-quality capacitors that frequently leak and degrade, often causing total system failure over time.
- Atari Jaguar units suffer from a poorly designed, dust-prone cartridge bus and unreliable CD add-on hardware.

### Why it Matters
These hardware defects turn once-valuable gaming systems into ticking time bombs that require specialized technical repairs to remain functional. Understanding these inherent flaws helps collectors avoid costly investments in failing hardware and encourages the use of more reliable, later-generation revisions or modern emulation alternatives.

articles.title: These 4 free open-source apps fix what Google left broken on my Pixel phone
articles.author: Chandraveer Mathur
articles.content:
Google’s Pixel lineup represents the pinnacle of Android, refined by years of software iteration. Unfortunately, the company may have lost focus on the core pillars of the user experience recently. Every year's Pixel experience seems incrementally better than the last, with most of the hardware bits like the camera sensors, shared, and AI tricks doing the heavy lifting. This makes Pixel devices resemble an on-ramp for Google's subscriptions more than a flagship Android phone.
Oftentimes, Pixel phones struggle with basics like looking up local files without ads or sending files to another OS easily. The solution comes from the unlikeliest corner of the internet — free and open-source (FOSS) apps. While most Play Store solutions are free, you're paying with personal information and usage stats. In contrast, FOSS apps aggressively respect your privacy, and pack polished features far exceeding their ad-ridden Play Store brethren. Here are the four mainstays on my Pixel.
Fossify File Manager
Enough with locking basic features behind a Pro pricetag
Android leaves you free to tinker with the system files, but you need a file manager to access them. Google ships Files by Google preinstalled, and it was a fantastic, lightweight utility that recently morphed into a billboard with an unhelpful homepage. It's filled with prompts to clean out junk, notifications about your cloud storage, and a UI that emphasizes the company's recurring subscription payments, while basic folder navigation is tucked into the sidebar.
The fix is Fossify File Manager. If you remember Simple Mobile Tools before the project was bought out and commercialized, Fossify is its spiritual, open-source successor. For newer users, the appealing UI features an ad-free design that supports Android's Material You dynamic theming. There are no confusing tabs or aggressive cleaning prompts. Instead, the landing homepage shows the main storage directory. It doesn't even do network permissions, so local file metadata isn’t being silently cataloged for targeted advertising.
Fossify also caters to power users with robust root access right off the bat, offering system directories (/data, /system) without installing sketchy third-party root explorer apps. It also handles USB OTG drives flawlessly, and you can heavily customize the display layout. For once, I'm glad a file explorer isn't a service cosplaying as an app.
Fossify File Manager
Fossify File Manager is a free and private file manager utility for Android with intregrated support for root access and biometric authentication for sensitive files.
LocalSend
Because Quick Share took too long to catch up
Apple's the only other company with total control of hardware and software, and I'd argue it's gone further than Google with AirDrop, because it works reliably and is device-agnostic, for file transfers between Apple products. In 2024, Google and Samsung developed Nearby Share together into Quick Share, bringing Windows support and simplifying transfers between Android devices. However, that was too little, too late.
LocalSend has been around for longer, and is the go-to privacy-conscious alternative to dodging Big Tech overreach. No sign-up, no Bluetooth pairing, and no internet connection required. You just need to connect to the same Wi-Fi as the sender/receiver. It still uses a secure REST API and HTTPS encryption over LAN. You can set up persistent destination directories, configure quick-save settings to bypass manual approval, and even run it in the background on PCs.
LocalSend
LocalSend is a free, cross-platorm tool that unlocks high-speed wireless file transfers between devices irrespective of the operating system they are running. It relies on Wi-Fi, and is touted as the go-to alternative for AirDrop and Quick Share.
VLC Media Player
The undisputed king who made it to space
Android's default media playback situation is fragmented. Google Photos handles local video, but YouTube Music handles audio. Using these cloud-first apps for local movies or FLAC feels clunky and invasive, especially with Photos constantly bringing up Drive backup. I used MX Player for decades until the Amazon acquisition that made the free version a wannabe streaming hub.
VLC rightly earned a flight on the latest Artemis mission since it's the ultimate replacement. VLC can play practically any media file without codec errors, including weirdly encoded MKVs and ancient AVIs. The intuitive interface retains swipe controls for volume, seeking, and brightness that are muscle memory.
This FOSS app shines with network streaming too. I also use VLC to stream video from my home NAS or PC over SMB and FTP. It effortlessly handles hardware decoding, PiP (picture-in-picture) mode, and a granular graphic equalizer, too.
VLC
VLC is an iconic media player that supports an extensive codec library despite its small installed app footprint. The app also handles web streaming on demand.
F-Droid
The gateway to all this good stuff
The Play Store works for mainstream apps like VLC, but is hostile to certain utilities. Anything blocking ads system-wide, scraping useful data, or modifying low-level system behaviors eventually gets nuked. F-Droid is the complete opposite, and a cornerstone of my Pixel setup, housing free and open-source software.
For the casual FOSS user, F-Droid is a safe, ad-free storefront. You won’t accidentally download a crypto miner or a credential stealer since listings are community-vetted. Plus, it's a streamlined updater for apps like Fossify File Manager and LocalSend without Google's involvement.
For power users, F-Droid helps manage custom software repositories seamlessly, saving APK hunting effort on GitHub for every update. F-Droid is also the launchpad for indispensable, anti-commercial tools such as Termux and NewPipe. It automates the maintenance of a Pixel, breaking free from Google's stronghold.
F-Droid
F-Droid is an open source app marketplace that courts liberated services from all over, platforming wholesale forks of other services and open source apps.
A well-rounded portfolio of basics
You don't have to de-Google a Pixel, run custom ROMs, and pirate software. The FOSS community offers a middle-ground with smart substitutions for core utilities, making devices faster, private, and less annoying for daily use.
articles.summary:
Google Pixel users are increasingly turning to free and open-source software alternatives to replace ad-heavy, cloud-reliant default applications with more private, efficient, and user-focused utility tools.

### Key Points
- Fossify File Manager provides an ad-free, privacy-focused alternative to Files by Google with support for root access and Material You dynamic theming.
- LocalSend enables high-speed, cross-platform wireless file transfers over local Wi-Fi, serving as a secure, device-agnostic replacement for Quick Share and AirDrop.
- VLC Media Player offers comprehensive codec support and network streaming capabilities, avoiding the fragmented and cloud-integrated experience of Google’s native media apps.
- F-Droid acts as a community-vetted, open-source app marketplace that hosts utilities often restricted or unavailable on the mainstream Google Play Store.

### Why it Matters
These open-source alternatives allow users to reclaim control over their hardware by removing intrusive advertisements and unnecessary data tracking from core system functions. Adopting these tools provides a more streamlined, privacy-centric mobile experience without requiring complex modifications like custom ROMs.

articles.title: Why is Meta raising Quest headset prices? #tech
articles.author: AllToc
articles.content:
Why is Meta raising Quest headset prices?
Meta’s Quest price hikes: RAM shortage and supply constraints
Meta is increasing prices on its Quest 3 and Quest 3S models, attributing the move to an ongoing RAM shortage. The company’s change means consumers will see higher sticker prices starting Sunday, with the rationale grounded in higher component costs rather than changes to the hardware lineup itself.
This comes amid broader reporting that memory availability and pricing have become major pressure points across electronics. With AI-driven demand and longer-than-expected supply stabilization, companies have faced higher costs for DRAM and related components. Meta’s explicit linkage of Quest pricing to RAM reflects that the VR hardware market is not insulated from the same supply-chain constraints affecting PCs, smartphones, and data-center gear.
What to watch next
- Whether Meta provides any guidance on expected RAM supply improvements.
- Whether the price increases appear as one-time adjustments or the start of continued changes for future headset refreshes.
Why it matters
Quest headsets are positioned for mainstream consumer use, so price changes directly affect adoption curves. If component scarcity persists, the economic friction can make it harder for developers and platform ecosystems to scale user bases quickly.
The story also highlights how hardware pricing is increasingly tied to availability of specific memory components—particularly relevant for devices that balance graphics performance, on-device inference, and overall responsiveness. In short, the change is presented as supply-chain economics, not a feature tradeoff.
articles.summary:
Meta is increasing the retail prices of its Quest 3 and Quest 3S virtual reality headsets starting this Sunday due to ongoing global supply constraints for essential RAM components.

### Key Points
- Meta cited a persistent shortage of DRAM and related memory components as the primary driver for the price adjustments.
- The price hikes apply to both the Quest 3 and the more affordable Quest 3S hardware models.
- Increased demand for memory in AI development and data centers has contributed to rising costs across the electronics industry.
- The company confirmed that the price changes are based on supply-chain economics rather than updates to the headset hardware.

### Why it Matters
These price increases could slow the adoption rate of Meta’s VR hardware by creating economic friction for mainstream consumers and developers. The situation underscores how global memory shortages directly impact the affordability and scalability of consumer electronics that rely on high-performance computing components.

articles.title: Live: More Wall Street records, oil prices tumble, ASX on edge
articles.author: Stephen Letts
articles.content:
ASX edges higher despite Hormuz tensions, oil prices rebound, truckies have a big win on fuel costs — as it happened
The ASX 200 clawed back earlier, steeper losses to close marginally higher with demand for retailers and banks outweighing selling in energy stocks and miners.
Trucking contractors had a big win in the Fair Work Commission, with retailers and big employers ordered to help with rising fuel costs.
Look back at how the trading session unfolded, plus the day's financial news and insights from our specialist business reporters on our blog.
Disclaimer: this blog is not intended as investment advice.
Key Events
Live updates
Market snapshot
- ASX 200: +0.1% to 8,953 points (live values below)
- ASX 200 (Friday): -0.1% to 8,947 points
- Australian dollar: -0.3% to 71.45 US cents
- Asia: Nikkei +0.6%, Hang Seng +0.6%, Shanghai +0.4%
- Wall Street (Friday): S&P500 +1.2%, Dow +1.8%, Nasdaq +1.3%
- Europe (Friday): Dax +2.3%, FTSE +2.0, Eurostoxx +1.6%
- Spot gold: -0.8% to $US4,790/ounce
- Oil: Brent futures +5.4% to $US95.22/barrel, WTI futures +6.0% to $US88.91/barrel
- Iron ore (Singapore): +0.3% to $US107.00/tonne
- Copper (LME): +0.6% to $US13,349/tonne
- Bitcoin: -0.4% at $US74,354
Prices current at around 4:15pm AEST
Live updates on the major ASX indices:
New: Filters
Choose what information you see below by using filters
Goodbye
That's it for another busy day on the blog, thanks for your company.
We'll be back with all the news from overnight early tomorrow (ATM: Wall Street futures are pricing in a steady rather than panicky retreat).
If you need a finance fix before then the ABC business unit is the place for you.
The Business with Kirsten Aiken is on ABC News at 8:45pm AEST and after the Late News on ABC TV.
Podcasts? Yep, got them too. Today's Fuelcast with Carrington Clarke and Alan Kohler is up and running, and Carrington talks to Business Editor Michael Janda on ABC Business Daily.
Until next time, missing you already ...
LoadingKey Event
ASX edges higher despite growing Gulf tensions and rising oil prices
The ASX has clawed its way back early losses to eke out a marginal gain over the session.
Having been down as much as 0.5% on opening, the benchmark ASX 200 closed 0.1% higher at 8,953 points. The broader All Ordinaries was up a similar amount.
Despite a jump in oil prices, the energy sector was sold off, along with the miners in the basic materials sector, while retailers were in solid demand.
While oil futures jumped around 6%, oil and gas producers were dumped. Karoon Energy fell 6.5%.
Retailers and refiners were also hit with Viva Energy down 9.1% after releasing quarterly production figures and an update on the Geelong refinery fire.
The big miners all fell (Rio Tinto -0.7%). The exceptions were gold miners with the local CDI listing of Newmont up 2.1% and Northern Star up 0.3%.
The big four banks largely battled back over the day (CBA +1.1%) but NAB (-3.6%) remained unloved after announcing increased provisions for bad debts this morning.
Consumer discretionary stocks such as Lovisa (+4.5%) and Tabcorp (+6.5%) were in demand.
The consumer staples sector, home of the big supermarkets, also propped up the ASX.
Alcohol retailers Treasury Wine Estates and Endeavour Group gained 2.5% and 1.8% respectively.
Coles and Woolworths both gained more than 1%, while at the other end of the food chain, A2 Milk and Graincorp ended down 0.8% and 0.6% respectively.
The broad industrial sector did its share of heavy lifting with building and plumbing related stocks Reliance Worldwide, Fletcher Building and Reece all up more than 2%.
ASX 200 top movers list was headed by payments platform Zip Co (+7.7%).
Viva Energy was the bottom performer on the ASX 200, while uranium stocks Paladin (-5.4%) and coal miners New Hope (-5.1%) and Whitehaven (-4.4%) were all down in a general energy sell off.
Key Event
Spending switch putting discretionary retail under pressure
Retailers have been in demand today with the ASX discretionary (cyclical) sector up 1.3% and staples (non-cyclical) not far behind, up 1.2%.
Pull the lens back and it’s a different picture.
The staples index, principally the big supermarkets, is up 9.3% over the year, while the discretionary sector, which includes luxury and electrical goods, travel and gambling, is down 12% in value.
NAB’s Online Retail Sales Index published today shows retail spending was up a solid 3% in March, an acceleration largely driven by the staples.
“Grocery and liquor (+7.6%) was a clear standout in growth terms in the month, perhaps reflecting some precautionary behaviour by households to stockpile non-perishable items,” NAB chief economist Sally Auld said.
“While average spend per transaction increased, the strength in grocery and liquor sales seems to be a story of additional orders as volume, measured by number of transactions, increased more rapidly.”
UBS retail analyst Shaun Cousins says rising inflation is likely to drive a wider gap between the two retail sectors.
“We expect the shift of spending away from discretionary to staples to be uneven,” Mr Cousins said.
“Supermarket spend is set to increase due to higher inflation, with volumes up, albeit modestly (slightly less eating out but fewer discretionary items in the basket).
“Supermarkets are focused on rebuilding price trust (especially Woolworths) so are asking suppliers to absorb much of these costs but, given the quantum of inflation, this is unlikely to be sustainable.
“More than during COVID, supermarkets will need to absorb supplier cost increases (more likely fresh, less likely dry grocery), reducing gross margins, but we forecast supermarket prices to rise.”
So how are consumers likely to react? Mr Cousins pinpointed a number of likely behaviours.
- Consumers are likely to trade down to promotions and private label (especially lower $ gross profit entry level ranges)
- Shop more with discounters (e.g. Aldi)
- Favour larger-format supermarkets where trips can be consolidated (and, unlike COVID, not shop local and independent given weaker price perception)
- In non-food, it is mixed, as Bunnings & Chemist Warehouse have stronger value perceptions, but are not as conveniently located as supermarkets. Liquor demand, unlike food, is cyclical, amplifying existing structural volume headwinds, with off-premises more challenged (eg liquor stores), while on-premises (hotels) benefits from the socialisation theme. moderating downside.
FairWork makes 'historic order' on truckie fuel costs
The country's transport union has just been delivered a big win by the Fair Work Commission on truckie fuel shock compensation.
It's ordered that retailers, miners and other companies that employ sub-contract drivers, mum and dad trucking companies and entities that provide trucking services other must hold twice-monthly reviews of fuel prices and adjust their rates accordingly.
This is the first order of its kind under new laws brought in to help sub-contracters deal with fuel shocks.
Subbie drivers like those that drive trucks for supermarkets can get offered a head contracted price per kilometre or delivery for their services. Or they get a flat fuel mechanism.
"There's thousands of ways these contracts are done," Emily McMillan from the TWU just told this journalist.
As fuel prices and especially diesel has soared, that has left subbies working for less, with the Transport Workers' Union even describing truckies working at a loss.
“This is a historic order from the Fair Work Commission that, for the first time, puts obligations on the wealthy clients at the top of our supply chains to pay their fair share to the transport industry," TWU National Secretary Michael Kaine has just said in a press statement.
“Over the last few weeks drivers and transport businesses have outlined the dire circumstances they are facing with diesel costs, many already having to park up their trucks or rely on personal loans just to keep going."
The order comes into effect tomorrow.
Are you a subbie driver that's been struggling? Email our journalist on terzon.emilia@abc.net.au
US reporting season: Earnings upgraded, Tesla results due
The US reporting season has opened strongly with the S&P 500 now back above where it was before the US-Iran war started.
The rally has been built on upgraded earnings forecasts trumping (so to speak) the anxieties over the war.
Global strategist Gerard Minack says while news from the Gulf clearly still affects day-to-day price action, it seemingly hasn't affected analysts' optimism about earnings.
"Year-ahead S&P EPS forecasts have risen 2.3% over the past month. Normally US EPS revisions are negative: the average 1 month change is -0.5%," Mr Minack wrote in a recent note.
"The energy sector is leading the upgrades, both in the US and rest of world markets.
"It seems the energy shock is large enough to significantly boost year-ahead energy sector earnings, but not large enough to materially affect the cycle or significantly hit earnings elsewhere.
"Consumer stocks are being downgraded, but their EPS forecasts were falling before the war started. Technology stocks are also seeing significant upgrades."
Tesla (Wednesday) will be the first of the so-called Magnificent Seven mega-corporations to report this season.
Other companies to report include plane maker Boeing, semiconductor company Intel and consumer products maker Procter & Gamble. Heavyweights such as Microsoft, Alphabet and Meta report the following week.
Key Event
Wall Street futures heading south
Just a quick look at the screen and seems it Wall Street will be giving up a fair chunk of Friday's gains when the market opens tonight.
Oil on the other hand is rebounding from Friday's fall.
At 3:00pm AEST:
- S&P 500 futures: -0.6%
- Dow futures: -0.7%
- Nasdaq futures: -0.6%
- Brent crude futures: +5.5%
- West Texas Intermediate crude futures: +5.9%
Hopes of oil tankers resuming trade through the Strait of Hormuz scuttled again
The hope that oil tankers would again start steaming through the Strait of Hormuz was an ephemeral thing — not even lasting the time span between Wall Street closing on Friday and the ASX opening this morning.
Eighteen ships, including six tankers, managed to slip through the strait in the brief window of opportunity, but at least 8 of these vessels never made it past the US naval blockade due to being sanctioned by the US. One was seized by the US navy.
On Sunday, ship tracking data showed outbound vessels through the Strait of Hormuz fell to zero.
Veteran CBA commodities analyst Vivek Dhar says he sees that the situation deteriorated over the weekend casts a worrying shadow over potential US Iran peace talks this week.
"The current climate is starkly different to the optimism heading into the weekend, where US President Trump flagged a US Iran peace deal was imminent," Mr Dhar said.
"Brent oil and European gas futures have already opened materially higher this week as energy markets price in a longer conflict than just a few days ago.
"Brent futures are tracking around $US96/bbl after falling as low as ~$US86/bbl in intraday trading on Friday.
"However, current prices remain well below the $US110/bbl level that Brent futures were trading just prior to the announcement of the two-week US Iran ceasefire.
Mr Dhar says the fall in Brent oil futures since the two-week ceasefire crucially masks the deterioration in physical flows of oil and refined product through the strait since March.
"The US naval blockade now threatens the 3.8mb/d of oil and refined product (~3.6% of global demand) that transited the strait in March as Iran is unlikely to allow any maritime traffic through if their shipments are blocked by the US navy."
ICYMI: Kohler on why resource-rich Australia became dependent on foreign oil
Books, columns, podcasts, nightly market wraps and a regular Sunday night finance long-form video - it's hard to keep up with the ABC's one-person economic think tank, Alan Kohler.
(We tried to get him to fill on the blog over lunch, but he hasn't returned our call).
Anyway, if you missed the 7pm News last night, 1) ask yourself why 2) down below, you can check out AK's take on how resource-rich Australia became utterly dependent on foreign oil.
There's also great old stock footage featuring a fine array of Holdens, from back in the day when we weren't utterly dependent on foreign cars.
Loading...Jim Chalmers looking back to the future with tax changes
Hi there,
My usual self-promotional post on a Monday for the ABC Business Daily podcast,
Today I spoke with Carrington about one of my favourite topics, tax policy.
I am, as many of you will know, especially interested in housing taxation, so we spent a lot of time looking at possible reforms to the Capital Gains Tax discount.
The excellent Shane Wright from Nine Newspapers, with whom I often hang out during Reserve Bank lock-ups, has reported that the current favourite is a move back from a 50% discount to inflation indexation, as CGT was before Peter Costello and John Howard changed it in 1999.
For long-term investors, this would represent a modest increase in tax and, for some, even a tax cut.
However, for property flippers it would mean a big jump in tax rates.
We also dissected the rationale behind an instant tax deduction of $1,000, legislation for which is being released by the government today.
First proposed in the 2010 budget by Wayne Swan, it never happened. But it looks like financial year 2026-27 is its time to shine.
The producers reckon this is a really good episode, but happy to take feedback in the comments from the people who matter most — our audience.
Census 2026 not far away
The ABS is recruiting people to doorknock the homes of Australia for the Census.
I have a soft spot for this role because my Oma (grandmother) did this job shortly after arriving in Australia with English as her second language.
It involves answering questions about the Census and, in some cases, helping people fill in the form.
They need an army too: 16,000 people!
The Census does more than count the people in the nation; it's a valuable source of information for governments (Do we need to build bigger schools there?) business (Where should I open my play centre?) and the community.
The role pays $31.19 per hour, plus super.
Key Event
ACCI urging the government to delay pay day super
Australian Chamber of Commerce and Industry Chief Executive Officer Andrew McKellar has held a lunchtime press conference, calling for a delay in the introduction of pay day super for small business.
He says small businesses are under a lot of cashflow pressure caused by the war in the Middle East.
"What we are seeing is at the moment, only around half of all businesses that we are surveying say that they are prepared for the implementation of pay day super," he says.
"Forty-five per cent of businesses are saying that they are concerned that it will have a negative impact on their cash flow in the near term.
"That's something that we definitely don't need at this time, when many businesses are already facing significant increases in costs, whether that's the direct cost of fuel, or other costs in their supply chain."
Pay day super - where superannuation contributions will be paid at the same time as wages, rather than up to every three months, is set to take effect from 1 July this year.
And while Mr McKellar says he supports the principle of pay day super, he is asking the government to delay the implementation for six months, until 1st January 2027 for all businesses who have 25 employees or less.
"We're urging the government to provide some relief, to buy some time, provide some breathing space for small businesses who are under so much pressure at this point in time," he says.

ASX top 20 marginally lower, dragged down by miners, NAB
Time for a quick check of the ASX top 20, the twenty most valuable and widely held stocks on the local market
At 1:15pm AEST the ASX 20 index was marginally lower, despite 12 of the 20 making gains. The benchmark ASX 200 was flat.
The index was dragged down by an outsized fall of the number 3 bank, NAB (-3.8%), as well sharp falls by Woodside (-3.1%) and the miners Fortescue (-1%) and Rio Tinto (-0.9%).

"We think NAB enters this potentially more vulnerable period from a much stronger starting point than that seen in previous periods."
